skill.name: creating-claude-agents
skill.description: Guides users through creating, improving, and validating Claude Code agents with correct frontmatter, tool access, model selection, and agent structure.
skill.summary: Create and validate Claude Code agents with clear structure, focused personas, and appropriate tool permissions.
skill.author: AgentWorkforce

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review shows they are almost entirely Markdown code fences, schema examples, tool names, and documentation URLs. No prompt injection, executable script, command execution path, network request, or malicious intent was found in the single skill file. One low-severity issue remains: the skill exposes a local developer filesystem path in documentation.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged Bash and command references appear inside Markdown examples, allowed-tools documentation, and illustrative code snippets. The skill contains only SKILL.md and does not execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced Markdown examples or prose. No executable Ruby, shell script, or command invocation mechanism exists in the file tree.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Are Not Active Network Behavior
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are references to a schema and Claude Code documentation. They are not used by executable code and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear as documentation references in Markdown. There is no fetch, curl, WebFetch instruction, or data exfiltration flow around them.

low_findings.title: Local Developer Path Disclosed
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill includes an absolute local schema path with a developer username. This is low severity, but marketplace content should avoid publishing personal filesystem paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line contains an absolute path under /Users with a personal-looking account name. The impact is limited to information disclosure in documentation.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Alerts Are Wording False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The high-severity scanner alerts point to prose, Markdown schema examples, model names, and validation checklist text. No cryptographic implementation, hash comparison, system probing, or network reconnaissance command was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of representative flagged regions found documentation only. The single-file skill has no executable code path where weak cryptography or reconnaissance could run.

content.value_statement: Claude Code agents can fail when their frontmatter, persona, tools, or model settings are unclear. This skill gives structured guidance for creating focused agents that validate and work predictably.
actual_capabilities: Explains the required Claude Code agent file location and Markdown structure.
Defines valid frontmatter fields, including name, description, allowed tools, model, and agentType.
Guides persona, H1 heading, instructions, process, examples, and output format design.
Shows how to choose Claude models for simple, balanced, and complex agents.
Provides validation checklists and troubleshooting steps for common agent errors.
Compares when to use agents, skills, and slash commands.
limitations: Does not create or validate agent files automatically without user action.
Does not include an executable validator or test harness.
References external documentation that may change over time.
Contains one local schema path that should be generalized before publication.

use_cases.title: Create a New Claude Code Agent
use_cases.description: Use the guidance to draft a complete agent file with correct frontmatter, persona, process, and tool limits.
use_cases.target_user: Developers building custom Claude Code workflows

use_cases.title: Improve an Existing Agent
use_cases.description: Review an agent for unclear descriptions, broad tools, weak persona design, missing examples, or invalid model settings.
use_cases.target_user: Team leads maintaining shared AI assistants

use_cases.title: Resolve Agent Validation Issues
use_cases.description: Map common schema and formatting errors to concrete fixes for names, descriptions, headings, tools, and model values.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a first Claude Code agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code agent for [task]. Include the required frontmatter, a clear H1 title, a persona, and simple instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Tighten Tool Permissions
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of agent access controls.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent design for least-privilege tool access. Recommend only the tools needed for [workflow] and explain each choice.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Activation Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced refinement for agent descriptions and behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve this Claude Code agent so it activates for the right tasks. Strengthen the description, persona, process, examples, and output format.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Agent Portfolio
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across multiple shared agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate these Claude Code agents as a set. Identify overlap, unclear responsibilities, risky tool grants, validation problems, and consolidation opportunities.

output_examples.input: Create a code review agent for a TypeScript team.
output: A complete agent outline with a lowercase hyphenated name and targeted description.
A reviewer persona focused on TypeScript, React, security, and maintainability.
A least-privilege tool set for reading files, searching code, and checking git context.
A structured review process with severity categories and file reference guidance.

output_examples.input: My agent is not activating when I expect it to run.
output: A diagnosis checklist for file location, frontmatter syntax, required fields, and description clarity.
Suggested wording changes that make the activation context more specific.
A reminder to test with explicit agent invocation before relying on automatic routing.

output_examples.input: Decide whether this should be an agent, skill, or slash command.
output: A recommendation based on task length, tool needs, repeatability, and manual invocation needs.
A short explanation of the tradeoffs between persistent agents, contextual skills, and quick commands.

best_practices: Keep each agent focused on one clear responsibility with a specific activation description.
Grant only the tools required for the workflow and restrict shell access when possible.
Include a persona, process, examples, and output expectations so behavior is consistent.
anti_patterns: Creating broad helper agents that review, debug, refactor, test, and document everything.
Using vague descriptions that do not tell Claude Code when to invoke the agent.
Granting broad Bash or write access to agents that only need read-only analysis.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design Claude Code agents with valid structure, clear behavior, and appropriate tool access.

faq.question: Does it generate agent files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns, examples, validation rules, and troubleshooting guidance for creating the files.

faq.question: Which tools does an agent need?
faq.answer: The skill recommends granting only the tools required for the agent task. Read-only agents should avoid write and shell access.

faq.question: How should I choose a model?
faq.answer: Use a fast model for simple checks, a balanced model for most work, and a stronger model for complex reasoning.

faq.question: Can this help fix validation errors?
faq.answer: Yes. It lists common issues with names, descriptions, headings, model values, and tool spelling.

faq.question: When should I use a skill instead of an agent?
faq.answer: Use a skill for contextual guidance or reference material. Use an agent for specialized multi-step work with tools.
